title: Week 3 Lecture
body:
Acknowledgement of Country

I acknowledge the Bedegal people that are the Traditional Custodians of this land. I would also like to pay my respects to the Elders both past and present and extend that respect to other Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders who are present here today.

SOCIAL ENTREPRENEURSHIP PRACTICUM (COMM3030)

LECTURE – WEEK 3 Topic/s: Design Thinking & Introduction to Double Diamond Framework

This week: Design Thinking Double Diamond Project Scope

Problem Statement A problem statement is a clear, concise explanation of the problem or challenge you intend to solve. It is meant to give you, your team, and any other stakeholders clarity and focus about the problem. The statement identifies the problem's current state, its desired future state and how to close the gap between the two states. It should establish a context for your audience, define the problem within this context and propose a solution to the problem.

LOGIC MODELS – The detail…

What we Plan….. What we expect the results to be…..

Inputs Activities Outputs Outcomes Impact

How do we know if it is working?

Introduction to Design Thinking

Design Traditional Design Thinking Business Thinking (Social Thinking Impact)

Adapted from Horth & Vehar (2014)

TRADITIONAL BUSINESS THINKING

What is Design is a process that facilitates problem-solving and innovation. Design is an effective model for identifying and comprehending the relationship between problems and solutions, according to Brown and Wyatt (2010). It presents

Design a particular way of considering the needs of the people who will consume a product or service and the enabling infrastructure.

Thinking? Design provides a structured approach to solving complex problems.

The processes involved, they explain, enable a more human-centred outcome that is more about the consumer experience than a consumer product (2010).

DESIGN THINKING

DESIGN THINKING FOR SOCIAL IMPACT

How to Design Change • The fun bit … • What is design thinking? • How does design apply to Social change? • What approaches are there to design thinking?

Whatever the Problem, Design has the Answer

Why is Design Thinking important for social impact?

"Design thinking is centred on innovating through the eyes of the end user and as such encourages in-the-field research that builds empathy for people, which results in deeper insights about their unmet needs. This focus helps avoid the common problem of enthusiastic “outsiders” promoting inappropriate solutions and ensures that solutions are rooted in the needs and desires of the community."

Brown 2011 (Stanford Social Innovation Review)

Human-Centred

“Not only does [design “The mission of Design thinking] focus on creating Thinking is to translate products and services that observation into insights are human centered, but and insights into products the process itself is also and services that will deeply human.” (Brown improve lives.” (Tim and Wyatt 2010). Brown - IDEO CEO)

Inspiration The steps in Ideation

Implementation

design thinking

THREE STAGES

Reference: Brown and Wyatt (2010)

SIX STAGES

Reference: d. School Hasso Plattner Institute of Design at Stanford University (2019)

The double diamond framework

Double Diamond The British Design Council (2019) outlines four core principles for problem-solvers to adopt so that they can work as effectively as possible within the Double Diamond framework:

• Put people first. Start with an understanding of the people using a service, their needs, strengths and aspirations. • Communicate visually and inclusively. Help people gain a shared understanding of the problem and ideas. • Collaborate and co-create. Work together and get inspired by what others are doing. • Iterate, iterate, iterate. Do this to spot errors early, avoid risk and build confidence in your ideas.

So what is it?

TWO STAGES - THE DOUBLE DIAMOND

The Two Diamonds The Double Diamond represents two important phases of design. The first diamond, "solving the right problem", includes discovering and understanding the challenge and defining the challenge further into specifics that can produce ideas to be tested. The second diamond, "solve the problem right", includes developing ideas and prototypes/tests that can be delivered to stakeholders to get feedback and refine these further.

Collaborate Meet with your team and key stakeholders

Determine what resources or help you may need

Brainstorm potential collaboration partners

Who might mutually benefit from collaboration?

Tailor your pitch for each potential collaborator

How do we use the Double Diamond process to deliver for our clients?

Problem Solution (Research) (Design)

'Doing the right thing' 'Doing things right'

Reference: British Design Council (2005)

Activity Ideas DISCOVER: Understand brief, primary research, secondary research, interviewing, empathy mapping, assumptions mapping, observation, mind mapping, brainstorming, systems maps.

DEFINE: Cluster learnings, share insights, generate 'how might we' questions, refine brief.

DEVELOP: Ideation, evaluate best ideas (dot-voting and impact/feasibility matrixes), prototype and test, business model canvases, integrate feedback.

DELIVER: Refine best ideas, synthesise.

This is not an exhaustive list. Activities for your schedule will vary depending on the nature of your brief and most importantly, your deliverables. Use these as a starting point!

Design mind set principles Empathy Optimism Iteration Creative Confidence Making Embracing Ambiguity Learning from Failure

IDEO.org

Pitching your concept Define the core idea of your product, experience or service.

What is your call to action?

Be clear and unambiguous - focus on the impact

Think about your audience – what do they need to know?

Write your pitch in appropriate format – presentation, story, video, report.

Practice your delivery or test out your pitch

Leadership is a very complex concept that is often confused with charisma and the use of power and authority. The truth is that it is all about enabling other people to be successful – and that requires, first, treating every person with unconditional respect and, second, the use of a very broad set of behaviours that enable people to grow and develop. It is about engaging with people.

DAC Model

About Week 4 In Class Time of week 4 you will be presenting the draft of your presentation to a representative of Surf Lifesaving Sydney This is a group exercise, and every member of your group must do part of the presentation You will then need to use the feedback you receive to finalise your group report that comprises your Assessment 2. This is due on February 4.

REMEMBER: in Week 5, (February 6) you need also to submit your Course Reflection Assessment
